title: Battle Hymn of the Tiger Mother
author: Amy Chua

chapters.title: All you know about severity is a warm-up for Chinese parents
chapters.text:
How are Western parents different from Chinese parents? First of all, according to statistics, child prodigies, musical or mathematical geniuses, are more likely to grow up in families of Chinese parents. Secondly, there are considerable differences in parenting systems. The book's author, Professor Amy Chua, recalls that she forbade her daughters to:

• Go to sleepovers with friends and participate in school plays.
• Watch TV or play computer games.
• Get grades below A.

When Western people think they are raising children in strictness, they are not even close to the level of Chinese mothers. Some parents believe they are strict when forcing their children to play music for an hour a day, but, in China, the first hour of a class is just a warm-up. Western parents often focus on the athletic performance of their children, while these achievements are secondary to the Chinese. A Chinese child must be the best in the class, and the only medal they should strive for is gold. In addition, mothers do not praise their children in public and always take the teacher’s side when they scold them.

It's not enough to be just successful when you can be the best.

Amy Chua has always been proud to be an authentic Chinese mother. She and her husband, Jed Rubenfeld, raised two daughters — Sophia and Louisa. Calm and attentive, Sophia had impressive abilities since childhood. At the age of one and a half, she knew the alphabet and could solve complex math problems in nursery school. When Sophia was three years old, she began to play the piano. At the same time, her younger sister Louise (Lulu) was utterly different from Sophia — hot-tempered and stubborn like her mother. Chua had prepared the fate of a violinist for Lulu.

What Chinese parents understand is that nothing is fun until you're good at it. ~ Amy Chua

Amy Chua was born in the year of the Tiger and compares herself to this animal — fearless, strong, and domineering — hence, in the Tiger Mother, she describes the conflicts with her daughters, their victories, the joys and sorrows of their family, and all the methods (sometimes shocking) of raising children. This summary will help you understand how children in Chinese families become geniuses through hard training and why pursuing excellence can sometimes ruin a mother-daughter relationship.

chapters.title: Music is the art of discipline
chapters.text:
Amy Chua spent her childhood in severity: she and her three younger sisters constantly studied mathematics, played the piano, and strove to get only the highest marks. Despite the severity, the family built relationships on respect and trust. The third generation of Chinese expatriates in America (like Chua's daughters) was born in comfort; these kids are more American than Chinese.

But Amy Chua didn't want her children to be sissies. She taught them hard physical labor and discipline. Chua decided that music would make her daughters more self-controlled, so she selected a teacher for Sophia who taught piano lessons using the Suzuki system. According to this method, parents should be present at every class. Under the supervision of her mother, five-year-old Sophia worked for at least an hour and a half every day, seven days a week. Chua recalls that she threatened to burn Sophia's toys if she did not play better. When Sophia was nine, she won a piano competition performing music by Edvard Grieg.

Direct all your thoughts and actions towards practice until you succeed.

Lulu also started playing the piano and turned out to be a born musician. Unlike Sophia, she was not so technical but had exceptional artistry. However, Chua later decided that her youngest daughter should play the violin.

Chua recalls comparing her daughters a lot — one of Chinese parents' features. She constantly used her eldest daughter as an example for her youngest. Thus, Chua pushed her youngest daughter to struggle with internal complexes. After a three-hour class with a teacher, Chua forced Lulu to keep playing at home to show a brilliant result in the next lesson. Chua's husband dreamed of making his daughters' childhood more fun by going to the water park or playing board games. But Chua believed that her children did not study enough as children can study for 10 hours a day in China.

Amy Chua used her strict educational system to prepare her daughters for a successful future, and, as a result, the sisters united in the fight against their overbearing mother. So, always remember that your children will not stop loving you if you bring them up in severity. But, it will be difficult for them to appreciate the virtue of your plans while they are small; be prepared for the fact that they will start looking for allies.

chapters.title: Chinese parents' shocking severity hides their desire for a better future for their children
chapters.text:
According to Amy Chua, Chinese parents sometimes allow themselves to abuse their children. For example, some Chinese mothers tell their daughters that they are fat and need to lose weight, and, one day, Chua's father called her "garbage." She still knew that her father loved her and just wanted her to never stop improving. However, such methods seem shocking to most families, as there are many other methods to express support.

Parents in China can demand high marks from their children because they believe they can receive them. They praise their children only behind closed doors and not in the presence of others. Finally, Chinese parents believe that their children owe them, so the children must pay back the debt to their father and mother all their lives.

Surprisingly, Chua's husband, who grew up in complete freedom, shared her strict model of raising their daughters. It led to impressive results; for example, when Sophia was 10 years old, she got the opportunity to perform with the orchestra in Yale University's Battell Chapel. Before the concert, they practiced day and night, and Sofia looked incredibly professional and elegant during the performance.

Never complain or make excuses. If something seems unfair, just prove yourself by working twice as hard and being twice as good. ~ Amy Chua

It was not so easy with Lulu. When she was seven, she continued to play the piano and violin and learned the piano piece The Little White Donkey. The girl did not want to study and even tore up her score. Chua threatened to leave her daughter without lunch and dinner, holiday gifts, and birthday parties in response to her outbursts and did not allow her to get up to drink water or go to the toilet. As a result, Lulu played well; she was happy and even forgot about the quarrels with her mother.

The worst thing parents can do when raising children is to give up.

Regardless of the level of strictness in which you decide to raise your children, do not forget to praise them; your child must see that their efforts are not in vain. Remember that excessive demands and lack of rest will not have the best consequences for your child in most cases.

chapters.title: Chinese parents are strict even with pets
chapters.text:
The girls’ parents always took their daughters with them when they visited different countries; they wanted the girls to experience and be influenced by other cultures. Chua’s parents and her sister Cynthia traveled with them, but they did not take a break from musical practice, even during trips. The family took Lulu's violin with them, and Sophia played the piano in hotels or restaurants. The girls' teachers were amazed at their level of training after the holidays. Thus, if your family can afford the practice for children during holidays or trips abroad, use the opportunity to study at least 15-30 minutes a day. Your children will learn to bring any business to perfection and approach work responsibly.

The most important part of upbringing for the Chua family was constant training. Once when Sophia took second place in a multiplication speed test, Chua forced her to solve twenty practical tests (of 100 problems each) every evening, standing next to her with a stopwatch. Since then, the girl has consistently ranked first. Lulu also had to practice constantly. She was preparing for a concert and could not learn the cadence (a part of the music piece closer to the finale when the soloist plays without the orchestra). Chua offered her daughter a deal — if she played the cadence well, she would buy her a dog. Lulu played brilliantly, and the family had a new member — a Samoyed named Coco.

You should reward your children’s efforts no matter how strict you are.

Coco was not a highly intelligent dog. The whole family wanted to walk and play with her, but she was rebellious. So, Chua decided to educate and train her. She read that Samoyeds are independent but quite smart and hard-working. Therefore, she determined that the reason for Coco's disobedience lay in stubbornness and tried to train her by applying the Chinese parenting model. But over time, she had to come to terms with Coco's character and stopped training her. Although untrainable, the dog brightened the family's lives, so Chua decided that it was enough for her that the dog was happy.

chapters.title: Life with a perfectionist mother and other problems
chapters.text:
In 2006, Chua's mother-in-law developed an acute form of leukemia. Florence Rubenfeld was a gorgeous woman, bright and optimistic, like a movie star. After chemotherapy, she needed care and moved to Chua's house. At first, Chua's husband was not happy about this idea because the women’s characters did not match, mainly due to the methods of raising children. Rubenfeld wanted a happy and carefree childhood for her granddaughters, while Chua did not know how to enjoy life at all. But for the Chinese, parents are the most important people; therefore, the usual strict mode of their children's life has not changed.

Rubenfeld seemed to be recovering quickly and was shining on Sophia's Bat Mitzvah. However, she died a week after returning to her New York apartment. Sophia and Lulu made heartwarming short speeches at the funeral calling their grandmother cheerful and joyful.

Chinese parenting is not related to the concept of happiness.

On the eve of the funeral, Chua asked her daughters to write short speeches. But she was not satisfied with their first drafts and said they were terrible. It made Sophia hysterical, but she rewrote the speech, and the new version was great. Previously, Chua’s daughters gave her homemade postcards on her birthday. She criticized these cards for their lack of effort. Chua stated that she wrote poetry to her mother as a child for her mother’s birthday, and got up at dawn to clean up and prepare breakfast. That evening, she received two delightful new postcards. Chinese parents are strict with their children no matter the circumstances because they know that life will test them in any mood.

The summer after Florence's death was difficult, but life went on, and the girls continued to earn more achievements in music. For example, Chua's friend heard Sophia play one day and invited her to audition for the piano department of the Yale School of Music. Lulu also continued practicing and got recognition from the famous musician Almita Vamos: she noted that the girl was naturally musical, but her technique was not professional enough. She said that Lulu could audition for Juilliard Preparatory School in a year.

Did you know? Bat Mitzvah is the coming-of-age ritual in Judaism for 12-year-old girls. From that date, the girls have the rights and obligations of a Jewish adult.

chapters.title: The ups and downs of performing in famous halls
chapters.text:
Sophia's audition at Yale School of Music was successful — she became the youngest student of Professor Wei-Yi Yang at just 14 years old. She began to learn an excerpt from Prokofiev's suite to Romeo and Juliet for a competition and diligently practiced for two months. After some time, the family received good news — Sophia became the first prize-winner of the competition and could perform at Carnegie Hall.

Lulu was not inferior to her older sister in success and was preparing with might and main for an audition at Juilliard, although this did not bring her pleasure. Chua's parents and husband could not believe that Lulu could be a professional violinist because the girl resisted too much. Although their mother's approach was the same, the daughters reacted differently to it and it was evident in their successes. So, keep in mind that your strict upbringing can interfere with the unshakable characteristics of even small children, so, treat them with respect.

Sophia's concert went brilliant; she performed masterfully and even allowed herself to improvise. However, luck did not submit to both sisters. On the day of her audition, Lulu suffered from severe food poisoning and could barely walk. Still, her parents took her to Juilliard, where she performed bravely. Unfortunately, Lulu was not admitted to the school that year, making her angry. Lulu even said that she hated Juilliard. But that evening, Chua received a call from violinist Naoko Tanaka, who was on Juilliard's jury. She offered Lulu a place in her private studio, and Chua accepted the invitation on behalf of her daughter.

Later, the sisters got the opportunity to perform together at the Museum Night in Budapest. The concert was supposed to take place in a month, so Chua threw all her strength into preparation.

The day before the performance, the family arrived in Budapest and attended a lesson with the famous violinist Mrs. Kazinczy, who was nervous and strict, constantly criticizing. In the end, Lulu's patience ran out, and she left the lesson in tears, saying that she would not return. Chua tried to persuade her daughter, appealing to her inner strength, but it did not help. Back at the hotel, she scolded Lulu because the Chinese always respect professionals’ authority. Still, even if you are sure that you are right, listen to the thoughts and feelings of your children. They will be grateful for your attention, even if they end up not listening to you.

You have to be strong to deal with difficulties, but it takes power to step back.

chapters.title: The Chinese parenting system fails with a rebellious teenager
chapters.text:
The sisters performed together and delivered a very successful duet. However, after the lesson with Kazinczy, Chua traced irreversible changes; Lulu rebelled. She realized that nothing terrible would happen if she refused to carry out her mother’s and teacher’s instructions. Even though Lulu succeeded and earned the concertmaster position in the youth orchestra at just 12 years old, she continued to rebel against everything her mother imposed on her, being rude to her music teacher and Chua and becoming distant and touchy. Therefore, consider that strict methods that once worked with even the most obedient children may not work with them in adolescence.

Lulu staged boycotts all the time. Once, she said that she wanted to have a haircut, to which Chua replied that she would not take her daughter to the salon because of her bad behavior. In response, Lulu cut her hair at home with stationery scissors, and it looked terrible. Also, she refused to play the violin at her Bat Mitzvah, although she was serious about her faith and waited for this event impatiently.

Growing up is impossible to control, and teenage rebellion is part of personality formation.

The period of Lulu’s rebellion was hard. Chua had to deal with her daughter’s difficult character and sad news from her sister Katrin. She said that she had been diagnosed with a rare form of leukemia and had to undergo chemotherapy and a bone marrow transplant. It was a difficult period, but Katrin was determined to live for her children. A bone marrow donor was eventually found, and Katrin began to recover. Despite the problems, a joyful event also happened in Chua’s family; they decided to buy another Samoyed, Pushkin, and she turned out to be even more beautiful than Coco.

Eventually, Lulu played the violin on her Bat Mitzvah to the delight of her guests. Two days after the event, while the family was having dinner in a restaurant in Russia, Chua and Lulu once again had an argument, and the girl screamed that she hated her mother. Chua burst into tears and ran away from the restaurant. She realized then that the moment had come, and she retreated. When she returned, she told Lulu that her violin studies were over. So, be prepared that a turning point may come up in your strict parenting system. It will take wisdom from you to know when to give in to your child and when to let them choose.

chapters.title: Conclusion
chapters.text:
Chua decided that her daughter's love was paramount to her. She remembered how her father hated his parents because they did not respect his personality and choices. Lulu left the orchestra; she did not give up the violin but did not practice regularly. In addition, she began to play tennis actively and amazed the coaches with her tenacity. She liked this sport, trained hard, and even started to win in tournaments.

Sophia continued to play the piano; she was happy making music. Looking at her performances, Amy Chua felt proud. She became more loyal and open to her daughters.

At the book’s first publication, Sophia and Lulu were 18 and 15, respectively. Amy Chua's daughters admitted that they enjoyed their childhood and did not regret their mother’s decisions, although they had to study a lot. Chua remained confident that children should be brought up with strictness, but this harshness should not get in the way of true love, even if you are a Chinese mother.

The Chinese education system is not for everyone. You may not be ready to constantly control your children and forbid them everything in the world - for many, friendship with their children is more important. Think about how you want to express your love and listen to your kids' needs. You are already an authority for your children, so sometimes simple support and love are enough.

Try this

Try the Shinichi Suzuki system if you want your kids to study music. It practices the techniques of early musical development, constant repetition, listening to the music every day, graded repertoire, and continuous encouragement. The course relies on seven textbooks, each with 10 to 15 sections, to be taught in order.

Sometimes, it is enough to give your children a choice to support them.

Read it till the end?
